question: Tell me about a time you had to make a difficult decision with incomplete data. (Bias for Action)
summary: Amazon Leadership Principle behavioral question testing comfort with calculated risk-taking and ownership.
whatTheyTest: Leadership Principle 'Bias for Action' — Amazon wants engineers who take calculated risks and move forward without waiting for perfect information.
explanation:
Amazon interviews are explicitly mapped to Leadership Principles (LPs). 'Bias for Action' tests whether you can move decisively when data is imperfect. The ideal answer demonstrates that you defined what was knowable quickly, identified the reversibility of the decision, calculated the cost of delay vs the cost of a wrong decision, and acted — with a plan to course-correct if needed.

Reversibility is key to Amazon's thinking: Jeff Bezos famously divided decisions into Type 1 (irreversible, slow down) and Type 2 (reversible, move fast). A strong answer shows you classified the decision correctly. If it was a Type 2 decision, you should have moved faster than if it were Type 1.

The STAR format is mandatory but Amazon interviewers push hard with follow-up 'Tell me more about what you considered' questions. Prepare 2-3 genuinely difficult situations, not softball scenarios where the answer was obvious in hindsight.
strongAnswerPoints: Classify the decision as reversible (Type 2) or irreversible (Type 1) and justify your speed accordingly.
Describe the specific data you had, the data you wished you had, and how you decided to proceed anyway.
Discuss the outcome and what monitoring/rollback plan you put in place to catch a wrong decision early.
whatToAvoid: Do not describe a situation where someone else made the final call — Amazon values individual ownership and autonomous decision-making.
ytQuery: Amazon Leadership Principles Bias for Action interview STAR

question: Design Amazon's product recommendation engine.
summary: System design for a collaborative filtering recommendation service at Amazon's scale serving millions of product suggestions daily.
whatTheyTest: Understanding of collaborative filtering vs content-based filtering, offline model training pipelines, real-time feature stores, and low-latency serving.
explanation:
Amazon's recommendation system has two distinct paths: offline training and online serving. In the offline path, user-item interaction data (purchases, clicks, views, ratings) is collected in a data lake (S3) and processed by a batch pipeline (Spark/EMR) to train a collaborative filtering model (matrix factorization or two-tower neural network). The model produces item embeddings — dense vectors where similar items are geometrically close.

In the online serving path, a user's request arrives at the recommendation service. A feature store fetches the user's recent behavior (last 5 clicks, current cart items) in real time. The system performs approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search using FAISS or ScaNN to find the top-k most similar items to the user's profile vector. Candidate items are then re-ranked by a lightweight scoring model that applies business rules (price, margin, stock level, sponsored items).

Caching is critical: pre-computed recommendations for all users are stored in ElastiCache (Redis) and refreshed every few hours. Real-time signals (the user just clicked X) personalize the cached set via a lightweight re-ranking step. The system must handle cold start for new users (fallback to popularity-based recommendations) and new items (content-based features).
strongAnswerPoints: Separate the offline training pipeline (Spark + matrix factorization) from the online serving path (ANN search + re-ranking).
Use a feature store for real-time user signals and pre-compute embeddings for approximate nearest neighbor retrieval.
Address cold start: new users get popularity-based recommendations; new items use content features until enough interactions accumulate.
whatToAvoid: Do not describe a fully real-time training loop — training happens offline in batches; only feature retrieval and scoring are real-time.
ytQuery: Amazon recommendation system design collaborative filtering

question: Find the Kth largest element in an unsorted array without full sorting.
summary: Selection algorithm using a min-heap or QuickSelect to find the Kth order statistic in optimal time.
whatTheyTest: Heap data structure usage, QuickSelect algorithm, and the ability to choose between O(n log k) heap vs O(n) average QuickSelect.
explanation:
The naive approach sorts the array (O(n log n)) and returns the Kth element from the end. But we can do better. A min-heap of size K maintains the K largest elements seen so far. Iterate through the array: push each element onto the heap. If the heap size exceeds K, pop the minimum. After processing all elements, the heap's root is the Kth largest. Time: O(n log k), Space: O(k).

QuickSelect achieves O(n) average time. It uses the same partition logic as QuickSort: pick a pivot, partition the array so all elements left of pivot are larger and all elements right are smaller (for descending order). If the pivot lands at index K-1, return it. If it lands at index > K-1, recurse left. If < K-1, recurse right. Average case is O(n) because each call processes approximately half the remaining elements.

In interviews, the heap solution is often preferred for its simplicity and guaranteed O(n log k) worst case. QuickSelect has O(n^2) worst case (bad pivot) but O(n) average. For streaming data (where elements arrive one at a time), only the heap approach works.
strongAnswerPoints: Min-heap of size K gives O(n log k) time — always know the Kth largest is the heap root.
QuickSelect gives O(n) average by recursing only into the relevant partition after each pivot.
For streaming or online data, only the heap approach is applicable — mention this distinction proactively.
whatToAvoid: Do not sort the entire array — it misses the point of the problem and shows you are not thinking about complexity.
ytQuery: Kth Largest Element heap QuickSelect LeetCode Amazon

question: Design Amazon's order management and fulfillment system.
summary: End-to-end system design for routing customer orders through warehouses, logistics, and last-mile delivery.
whatTheyTest: Knowledge of event-driven architecture, saga pattern for distributed transactions, and the complexity of coordinating multi-step fulfillment workflows.
explanation:
Order management involves multiple distributed services: inventory, payment, warehouse management, shipping, and notifications. A naive synchronous chain of API calls fails under load and creates tight coupling. The correct architecture uses event-driven design with a message broker (Amazon SQS/SNS or Kafka).

When a customer places an order, an OrderPlaced event is published. Downstream services consume this event asynchronously: the Payment service charges the card, the Inventory service reserves stock, the Warehouse service creates a pick-pack-ship job. Each step publishes success or failure events. This is the Saga pattern: a sequence of local transactions coordinated via events rather than a distributed ACID transaction.

For failures mid-saga (e.g., payment succeeds but inventory is out of stock), compensating transactions must reverse prior steps (issue a refund). The order state machine tracks each order through states: PENDING → PAYMENT_CONFIRMED → INVENTORY_RESERVED → SHIPPED → DELIVERED, with terminal states CANCELLED and FAILED. Status updates are pushed to the customer via SNS/email/SMS. Idempotency keys ensure that retried events don't double-charge or double-reserve.
strongAnswerPoints: Use event-driven architecture (Kafka/SQS) to decouple services — avoid synchronous chains that create cascading failures.
Apply the Saga pattern with compensating transactions to handle partial failures across payment, inventory, and shipping.
Model order as an explicit state machine (PENDING → CONFIRMED → SHIPPED → DELIVERED) to make transitions auditable.
whatToAvoid: Do not design a single monolithic service handling all steps — explain why distributed services with events are necessary at Amazon's scale.
ytQuery: Amazon order management system design saga pattern event driven

question: Describe a time you dived deep into a problem and found a root cause others had missed. (Dive Deep)
summary: Amazon LP behavioral question testing analytical depth and persistence in investigating complex technical problems.
whatTheyTest: Leadership Principle 'Dive Deep' — ability to go beyond surface symptoms to find root causes using data, metrics, and systematic investigation.
explanation:
Amazon's 'Dive Deep' principle specifically tests whether you avoid staying at a high level when a problem demands granular investigation. The interviewer wants to hear about logs, metrics, traces, query plans, memory dumps — specific technical artifacts that led you to a non-obvious root cause.

The best answers involve a situation where the obvious hypothesis was wrong. You checked the obvious thing first, it didn't explain the data, you dug one layer deeper, and kept going until you found the true root cause. This might mean analyzing a flame graph, writing a custom query against access logs, or reading source code of a third-party library.

Amazon interviewers will probe: 'How did you know that was the root cause and not just a symptom?' Prepare to explain your verification method — how you confirmed the root cause and reproduced or observed the issue in isolation.
strongAnswerPoints: Name specific technical tools and artifacts you used: logs, metrics dashboards, flame graphs, explain plans, packet captures.
Describe at least two wrong hypotheses you eliminated before finding the root cause — this shows depth.
Explain how you verified the root cause was correct, not just plausible, before proposing a fix.
whatToAvoid: Do not describe a situation where the root cause was immediately obvious or where someone else found it — this tests your personal analytical rigor.
ytQuery: Amazon Dive Deep Leadership Principle behavioral interview

question: Two Sum: find indices of two numbers that add up to a target.
summary: Foundation hash map problem testing O(n) lookup by storing complement-to-index mappings.
whatTheyTest: Basic hash map mastery and the ability to optimize from O(n^2) brute force to O(n) with a single pass.
explanation:
The brute force approach uses two nested loops to check all pairs — O(n^2). The optimal approach uses a hash map to store each number's index. For each element, compute its complement (target - current). If the complement exists in the map, return both indices. Otherwise, add the current number to the map. This achieves O(n) time and O(n) space with a single pass.

The key insight is that you don't need to see both numbers at the same time. When you process the second number of the pair, the first number is already in the hash map. This 'look back' pattern generalizes to many other problems.

Common follow-ups at Amazon: What if the input is sorted? (Use two pointers, O(1) space.) What if you need all pairs, not just one? (Collect all valid pairs.) What if numbers can be used twice? (Check complement != current index.) Always clarify these constraints upfront.
strongAnswerPoints: Store complement-to-index in a hash map and look up the complement for each element in O(1).
Single pass is sufficient — when you find a complement, both indices are immediately available.
Discuss follow-ups: sorted array (two pointers), all pairs, no duplicate usage — shows breadth.
whatToAvoid: Do not sort and use two pointers on the original unsorted array if indices must be returned — sorting destroys the original positions.
ytQuery: Two Sum LeetCode hash map optimal solution

question: Design Amazon S3 (Simple Storage Service).
summary: Design a globally available, durable, massively scalable object storage service.
whatTheyTest: Understanding of distributed blob storage, erasure coding, consistent hashing, metadata management, and multi-region replication.
explanation:
S3's architecture separates metadata management from data storage. Object metadata (bucket name, object key, size, checksum, permissions, storage class) is stored in a distributed key-value metadata store. The actual object bytes are stored across a fleet of storage nodes partitioned by consistent hashing.

For durability (11 nines: 99.999999999%), S3 uses erasure coding rather than simple replication. An object is split into data chunks and parity chunks (e.g., 6+3 erasure coding), distributing them across different storage nodes, racks, and availability zones. Any 3 nodes can fail and the object is still fully reconstructable. This is more storage-efficient than 3x replication.

Uploads above 5MB use multipart upload: the client splits the object, uploads parts in parallel, and S3 assembles them. This allows resumable uploads and saturates network bandwidth. For downloads, pre-signed URLs allow clients to download directly from S3 without proxying through your server. The metadata path (creating buckets, listing objects) uses a consistent, strongly consistent store (upgraded from eventual consistency in 2020).
strongAnswerPoints: Separate metadata store from data store — metadata needs strong consistency; data storage optimizes for throughput.
Use erasure coding (6+3) for 11-nines durability — more efficient than 3x replication at the same durability level.
Describe multipart upload for large files and pre-signed URLs for client-direct downloads.
whatToAvoid: Do not confuse S3 (object storage, flat namespace) with a file system (hierarchical, POSIX semantics) — they are fundamentally different.
ytQuery: Design Amazon S3 object storage system design

question: Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Search Tree.
summary: Find the LCA of two nodes in a BST by leveraging the BST ordering property for an efficient solution.
whatTheyTest: Understanding of BST properties and the ability to exploit ordering invariants to avoid unnecessary traversal.
explanation:
The LCA of two nodes p and q in a BST is the deepest node whose value lies between p and q (inclusive). Unlike a general binary tree where you need full traversal, the BST property lets you navigate directly: if both p and q are smaller than the current node, the LCA must be in the left subtree. If both are larger, it's in the right subtree. Otherwise, the current node is the LCA (it's where the paths diverge).

This iterative approach runs in O(h) time where h is the tree height — O(log n) for a balanced BST, O(n) for degenerate. No extra space is needed beyond the current node pointer.

For a general binary tree (not BST), the problem requires O(n) full traversal: recursively search left and right subtrees, returning the node when both p and q are found in opposite subtrees or when the current node is p or q.
strongAnswerPoints: Exploit the BST property: if both values are less than current, go left; both greater, go right; otherwise current is the LCA.
Iterative solution is O(h) time O(1) space — no recursion stack needed.
Contrast with the general binary tree LCA which requires O(n) traversal since there's no ordering to exploit.
whatToAvoid: Do not use a general binary tree LCA algorithm on a BST — you will miss the optimization and fail to demonstrate BST knowledge.
ytQuery: Lowest Common Ancestor BST iterative solution

question: Design Amazon's Inventory Management System for high-traffic flash sales.
summary: System design for preventing overselling, managing stock atomically, and handling traffic spikes during events like Prime Day.
whatTheyTest: Knowledge of distributed locks, optimistic vs pessimistic concurrency, Redis atomic operations, and queue-based load leveling for bursty traffic.
explanation:
The core challenge is preventing overselling: two customers must never both successfully purchase the last item. Optimistic concurrency control uses a version number on the inventory record. A read-modify-write is only committed if the version hasn't changed since the read. Under high contention this causes many retries. Pessimistic locking (SELECT FOR UPDATE) serializes access but kills throughput at scale.

The preferred approach at Amazon's scale uses Redis DECR as an atomic pre-check. When inventory > 0, DECR atomically decrements it. If the result is >= 0, proceed to create the order. If the result is < 0, increment it back and return out-of-stock. This keeps the hot path in memory. The authoritative inventory in the database is updated asynchronously in batches, with the Redis count resynchronized periodically.

For flash sales, a virtual queue (SQS FIFO) is placed in front of the checkout service. Customers are issued queue tokens and processed in order. This prevents thundering herd from overwhelming the database and provides a fair first-come-first-served experience. Real-time queue position is shown to the user.
strongAnswerPoints: Use Redis DECR as an atomic in-memory pre-check to avoid database contention for every request.
Implement a virtual queue (SQS FIFO) during flash sales to level the load and provide fairness guarantees.
Discuss the consistency model: Redis is the fast pre-filter; database is the authoritative source with eventual sync.
whatToAvoid: Do not rely solely on database-level locking for flash sale traffic — SELECT FOR UPDATE at millions of QPS causes severe lock contention.
ytQuery: Inventory management system design flash sale overselling prevention Redis

question: Tell me about a time you raised the bar on quality for your team. (Insist on the Highest Standards)
summary: Amazon LP behavioral question evaluating whether you proactively improve quality beyond what was asked.
whatTheyTest: Leadership Principle 'Insist on the Highest Standards' — Amazon wants engineers who hold themselves and their team to high standards and push back on mediocre work.
explanation:
This principle tests whether you are satisfied with 'good enough' or whether you actively raise the bar. Strong answers describe a concrete situation where an existing standard was inadequate, you identified the gap, proposed a better standard, and drove adoption — not just for yourself but for the team or organization.

Examples can include establishing code review practices, introducing observability standards (SLOs, error budgets), setting up better testing coverage thresholds, or pushing back on shipping a feature with known technical debt. The key is that you weren't asked to do this — you identified the need and took ownership.

Amazon interviewers will push back with 'Wasn't that just doing your job?' Differentiate by showing that the existing standard was genuinely accepted and that raising it required influencing others who were not immediately on board.
strongAnswerPoints: Describe the specific gap in the existing standard and use metrics or outcomes to show why it mattered.
Explain how you convinced others to adopt the higher standard — influence without authority is key.
Share the measurable impact: fewer bugs, faster deployments, reduced incidents — quantify where possible.
whatToAvoid: Do not describe simply fixing a bug or improving your own code — the standard must have been raised for others, not just yourself.
ytQuery: Amazon Insist Highest Standards Leadership Principle interview

question: Number of Islands: count distinct islands in a 2D binary grid.
summary: Graph connectivity problem using DFS or BFS to count connected components of '1' cells.
whatTheyTest: DFS/BFS graph traversal, in-place grid modification to track visited cells, and identifying connected components.
explanation:
Model the grid as a graph where each '1' cell is a node with edges to its 4 neighbors (up, down, left, right). Count the number of connected components. Start a DFS/BFS from every unvisited '1' cell, marking all reachable '1' cells as visited. Each time you start a new DFS, increment the island count.

The most space-efficient approach mutates the grid in place: when you visit a '1' cell, mark it '0' to prevent revisiting. This eliminates the need for a separate visited set. If the input must not be modified, use an explicit visited set or a second boolean matrix.

BFS and DFS are equivalent here. DFS with recursion can hit call stack limits for very large grids (use iterative DFS or BFS instead). Union-Find is a third approach that can process cells in parallel and is used in distributed grid processing.
strongAnswerPoints: Mutate '1' cells to '0' during traversal as an O(1) space way to mark visited cells.
Iterate every cell and start a DFS/BFS only when you find an unvisited '1' — each DFS call covers one complete island.
For very large grids, use iterative BFS or explicit stack DFS to avoid call stack overflow.
whatToAvoid: Do not use a recursive DFS without mentioning potential stack overflow for very large grids — propose the iterative alternative.
ytQuery: Number of Islands DFS BFS LeetCode grid traversal

question: Design Amazon's notification delivery system (email, SMS, push).
summary: System design for a high-throughput, multi-channel notification service with guaranteed delivery and deduplication.
whatTheyTest: Asynchronous processing via message queues, at-least-once delivery, idempotency, third-party provider integration, and preference management.
explanation:
A notification system separates the trigger (event that causes a notification) from the delivery (actually sending). The trigger service publishes events to a message broker (SNS/SQS). Downstream workers consume events and render templated messages (Handlebars/Jinja2 templates stored per notification type).

Delivery workers integrate with third-party providers: Amazon SES for email, Twilio/SNS for SMS, APNs/FCM for push. At-least-once delivery means a notification might be sent twice if a worker crashes after sending but before acknowledging the message. Idempotency tokens (stored in DynamoDB with a TTL) deduplicate deliveries — if the same token is seen twice, skip the second send.

User preferences (opt-out lists, quiet hours, channel preferences) must be checked before delivery. This preference lookup must be fast (Redis cache) since it's on the critical path. A priority queue handles transactional notifications (order confirmation) at higher priority than marketing emails. Failed deliveries are retried with exponential backoff and routed to a dead-letter queue for manual inspection after N retries.
strongAnswerPoints: Decouple event publishing from delivery using a message broker — the trigger service doesn't care about channels.
Use idempotency tokens stored in a fast key-value store to deduplicate at-least-once delivery.
Check user preferences from a Redis-cached preference store before each delivery to respect opt-outs and quiet hours.
whatToAvoid: Do not design a synchronous notification call in the order flow — notifications are async fire-and-forget and must not block the main transaction.
ytQuery: Notification system design SNS SQS email push SMS

question: Validate a Binary Search Tree.
summary: Verify that every node in a binary tree satisfies the BST invariant using valid min/max range propagation.
whatTheyTest: Understanding that BST validation requires tracking valid ranges, not just comparing a node to its immediate children.
explanation:
The common mistake is checking only parent-child relationships. Consider a tree where the right child of the root has a left child smaller than the root — this violates BST but passes a naive child-only check. The correct approach threads valid (min, max) bounds down the tree. At the root, both bounds are (-infinity, +infinity). When going left, the current node's value becomes the new max. When going right, the current node's value becomes the new min.

A node is invalid if its value falls outside its (min, max) range. Null nodes are valid. This approach correctly catches violations anywhere in the tree by propagating constraints from ancestors down to descendants.

An equivalent approach is to perform an inorder traversal and check that values are strictly increasing — a valid BST's inorder traversal is always a sorted sequence. This is O(n) time and O(h) space (recursion stack).
strongAnswerPoints: Thread (min, max) bounds down the tree — going left tightens the upper bound; going right tightens the lower bound.
Use float('-inf') and float('inf') as initial bounds to avoid special-casing the root.
The inorder traversal approach is equally valid: a BST's inorder output is strictly increasing.
whatToAvoid: Do not only compare each node to its direct children — a node's value must satisfy constraints from all its ancestors.
ytQuery: Validate Binary Search Tree LeetCode range propagation

question: Tell me about a time you had to earn trust with a skeptical stakeholder. (Earn Trust)
summary: Amazon LP behavioral question testing interpersonal credibility-building through transparency, follow-through, and data.
whatTheyTest: Leadership Principle 'Earn Trust' — Amazon values engineers who build trust through honest communication, admitting mistakes, and delivering on commitments.
explanation:
This principle tests your emotional intelligence and professional maturity. Amazon interviewers want to see that you understand trust is built through actions, not words. Strong answers involve situations where your credibility was low to start (new to a team, after a mistake, disagreement with a senior person) and you methodically rebuilt it.

Key behaviors Amazon looks for: being transparent about what you know and don't know, delivering on small commitments before big ones, acknowledging mistakes promptly without making excuses, seeking input rather than steamrolling, and using data to back up claims rather than relying on authority.

Avoid answers that frame the stakeholder as the problem. Even if they were being unreasonable, the best answer acknowledges their perspective and shows how you addressed their underlying concern rather than proving them wrong.
strongAnswerPoints: Describe the specific reason the stakeholder was skeptical — was it a past failure, a disagreement, or a new relationship?
Explain the concrete actions you took: transparency about risks, incremental proof points, asking for feedback regularly.
Share the evidence that trust was earned — changed behavior from the stakeholder, positive outcome, ongoing relationship.
whatToAvoid: Do not frame the situation as you being right and the stakeholder being wrong — the LP is about building trust, not winning arguments.
ytQuery: Amazon Earn Trust Leadership Principle behavioral interview STAR

question: Design a URL shortener service (like bit.ly) at Amazon scale.
summary: Classic system design for generating short URLs, storing mappings, and redirecting billions of requests.
whatTheyTest: Hash function design, base62 encoding, database schema, read-heavy caching strategy, and horizontal scaling.
explanation:
A URL shortener needs to: generate a unique short code for a long URL, store the mapping, and redirect users to the original URL quickly. For the short code, generate a random 7-character base62 string (62^7 = 3.5 trillion unique codes). Alternatively, use a hash of the long URL (MD5/SHA256) and take the first 7 characters — handle collisions by appending a counter.

The database stores (short_code, long_url, created_at, user_id, expiry). Short code is the primary key. Given that reads (redirects) vastly outnumber writes (URL creations), the system is heavily read-optimized. Cache the short_code → long_url mapping in Redis with no expiry for popular URLs. A cache hit serves the redirect in <1ms without touching the database.

For 100 billion redirects/day, the redirect service must be stateless and horizontally scalable behind a load balancer. The database can be sharded by short_code prefix or use a managed NoSQL store like DynamoDB with short_code as the partition key. Analytics (click counts, geo distribution) are computed asynchronously from a Kafka stream of click events.
strongAnswerPoints: Use base62 encoding on a 7-character random string or hash prefix for short codes — 3.5 trillion unique codes.
Cache short_code → long_url in Redis for read-heavy redirect path — target <5ms p99 redirect latency.
Separate the write path (URL creation) from the read path (redirect) with independent scaling strategies.
whatToAvoid: Do not use sequential integer IDs for short codes — they are guessable (security issue) and create a single-point write bottleneck.
ytQuery: URL shortener system design bit.ly Amazon interview

question: Number of Islands: count connected components of '1's in a 2D grid using DFS or BFS.
summary: Classic graph traversal problem counting distinct islands in a binary grid — heavily asked at Amazon.
whatTheyTest: Grid-as-graph modeling, DFS/BFS connected-component traversal, and in-place visited marking.
explanation:
Treat every '1' cell as a graph node with edges to its four horizontal and vertical neighbors. The task reduces to counting connected components. Iterate every cell; when you land on a '1', launch a DFS that sinks every reachable '1' cell to '0' (marking it visited in-place) and increment the island counter. Because sinking destroys the original grid, ask the interviewer if mutation is permitted; if not, carry an explicit boolean visited matrix instead.

DFS uses the call stack (O(min(R,C)) recursion depth for a snake-shaped island) while BFS uses an explicit queue of the same size. For very large grids, BFS is safer against stack overflow. A Union-Find approach assigns each '1' cell a parent and merges adjacent cells; the number of remaining roots equals the island count. Union-Find shines when cells arrive in a stream or in parallel.

Time complexity is O(R*C) since each cell is visited at most once. Space is O(R*C) for the recursion stack or BFS queue in the worst case where the grid is all '1's.
strongAnswerPoints: Sink visited '1' cells to '0' in-place to avoid a separate visited matrix — O(1) extra space per cell.
Use BFS with an explicit queue for large grids to avoid recursion stack overflow.
Union-Find is an alternative that handles streaming cell additions and parallel processing naturally.
whatToAvoid: Do not check all eight directions (including diagonals) unless the problem explicitly allows diagonal connections — standard islands use only four directions.
ytQuery: Number of Islands LeetCode BFS DFS Amazon graph

question: Minimum Window Substring: find the smallest window in string S that contains all characters of string T.
summary: Sliding window with a frequency map to shrink and expand a window until the shortest valid window is found.
whatTheyTest: Two-pointer sliding window pattern, frequency counting, and the shrink-expand strategy for substring problems.
explanation:
Maintain two pointers (left and right) over S and a frequency map of the characters still needed to satisfy T. Expand right one character at a time; when the current character satisfies a need, decrement the need count. Once all characters are satisfied (formed == required), try to shrink from the left to find the minimum window, updating the global best before moving left forward.

The key bookkeeping variable is 'formed': the number of unique characters in T that are currently satisfied in the window at the required frequency. When formed == required (number of unique characters in T), the window is valid. Shrinking from the left may break validity, so you expand again. This two-phase expand-then-shrink cycle ensures every character is visited at most twice — O(|S| + |T|) time.

Edge cases: T longer than S (return ''), duplicate characters in T (frequency must match, not just presence), and S containing no characters from T (return ''). An optimized variant filters S to only characters appearing in T first, reducing the number of steps when T is dense but S has many irrelevant characters.
strongAnswerPoints: Track 'formed' (satisfied unique character counts) so validity check is O(1) instead of re-scanning the window.
Shrink from the left as soon as the window is valid — this guarantees you find the minimum for each right position.
Handle duplicate characters in T by comparing window frequency against required frequency, not just presence.
whatToAvoid: Do not re-count the entire window on each step — maintain a running frequency map and a single 'formed' counter to keep the solution O(|S|+|T|).
ytQuery: Minimum Window Substring sliding window LeetCode Amazon hard

question: Reverse Linked List in K-Groups: reverse every k consecutive nodes of a linked list.
summary: Pointer manipulation problem requiring careful group-by-group reversal with reconnection of group boundaries.
whatTheyTest: Advanced linked list pointer manipulation, in-place reversal of sublists, and handling edge cases like incomplete final groups.
explanation:
First check that at least k nodes remain before reversing; if fewer remain, leave them as-is (per the standard problem statement). For each group of k: record the group_prev (node before the group) and group_start (first node of the group). Reverse k nodes using the standard three-pointer reversal. After reversal, group_start has become the tail of the reversed group — connect it to the next group's start. Connect group_prev.next to the new head of the reversed group. Advance group_prev to group_start and repeat.

A sentinel dummy node simplifies the edge case of reversing the very first group, because group_prev is never null — it starts as the dummy. This eliminates special handling for the head of the list.

Recursive approach: reverse the first k nodes, then recursively call on the remainder and attach the result to the tail of the reversed group. The recursion depth is O(n/k), which could overflow the stack for very large lists, making the iterative approach safer. Time is O(n), space is O(1) iteratively.
codeExample:
class ListNode:
    def __init__(self, val=0, next=None):
        self.val = val
        self.next = next

def reverse_k_group(head, k):
    dummy = ListNode(0)
    dummy.next = head
    group_prev = dummy

    while True:
        # Check if k nodes remain
        kth = get_kth(group_prev, k)
        if not kth:
            break
        group_next = kth.next

        # Reverse k nodes
        prev, curr = group_next, group_prev.next
        while curr != group_next:
            tmp = curr.next
            curr.next = prev
            prev = curr
            curr = tmp

        # Reconnect
        tmp = group_prev.next
        group_prev.next = kth
        group_prev = tmp

    return dummy.next

def get_kth(node, k):
    while node and k > 0:
        node = node.next
        k -= 1
    return node
strongAnswerPoints: Use a dummy node so group_prev is never null — simplifies the connection logic for the first group.
Check k nodes exist before reversing to leave an incomplete final group untouched.
Track group_next (node after the group) before reversing so the tail can be re-connected correctly.
whatToAvoid: Do not forget to re-link group_prev.next to the new group head after reversal — failing to do this disconnects the list.
ytQuery: Reverse Nodes in k-Group LeetCode linked list hard Amazon

question: Search in Rotated Sorted Array: find a target in a sorted array rotated at an unknown pivot.
summary: Binary search variant that determines which half is sorted at each step to correctly narrow the search range.
whatTheyTest: Modified binary search reasoning, identification of the sorted half, and handling the rotation invariant correctly.
explanation:
A rotated sorted array like [4,5,6,7,0,1,2] has the property that at any mid-point, at least one of the two halves is fully sorted. Compare nums[left] with nums[mid]: if nums[left] <= nums[mid], the left half is sorted. Check whether the target falls within [nums[left], nums[mid]]; if yes, discard the right half; otherwise discard the left half. If the left half is not sorted, the right half must be sorted — apply the same logic symmetrically.

This maintains the O(log n) guarantee of binary search even though the array is not fully sorted. The equality case in nums[left] <= nums[mid] handles the single-element subarray correctly.

A follow-up is finding the rotation pivot first (using binary search to find the minimum element), then running standard binary search on the correct segment. This is two passes but cleaner to reason about. The one-pass approach above is more elegant and preferred in interviews. If duplicates are present, worst-case degrades to O(n) because nums[left] == nums[mid] is ambiguous about which half is sorted.
strongAnswerPoints: At every mid, one half is always fully sorted — identify which half and check if target falls within its range.
The condition nums[left] <= nums[mid] (with equality) correctly identifies the sorted left half.
Mention that duplicates break the O(log n) guarantee because nums[left] == nums[mid] is ambiguous.
whatToAvoid: Do not try to find the pivot index as a separate step in the same pass — it complicates the logic; either find pivot first then binary search, or use the one-pass approach above.
ytQuery: Search in Rotated Sorted Array binary search LeetCode Amazon

question: Largest Rectangle in Histogram: find the area of the largest rectangle that fits entirely within a histogram.
summary: Monotonic stack approach that tracks bars that can extend leftward to find the maximum rectangle in O(n).
whatTheyTest: Monotonic stack pattern, the insight that each bar is bounded by the nearest shorter bar on each side, and O(n) stack-based optimization over O(n^2) brute force.
explanation:
For each bar, the largest rectangle it can be part of extends left until a shorter bar is hit and extends right until a shorter bar is hit. The width is (right_boundary - left_boundary - 1) and height is the bar's own height. The brute force O(n^2) approach checks every pair of bars. The stack-based O(n) approach maintains an increasing monotonic stack of bar indices.

When you encounter a bar shorter than the stack's top, the top bar can no longer extend rightward — pop it and calculate the rectangle: height = heights[popped], width = current_index - stack[-1] - 1 (or current_index if stack is empty). Push the current index. After iterating all bars, pop remaining stack entries against a virtual right boundary at index n.

Appending a sentinel bar of height 0 at the end simplifies the code by ensuring all bars are popped during the main loop rather than needing a separate post-loop cleanup. This is a classic example where recognizing the 'nearest smaller element' subproblem unlocks the O(n) solution.
codeExample:
def largest_rectangle_area(heights):
    heights.append(0)  # sentinel
    stack = []  # indices, monotonically increasing by height
    max_area = 0

    for i, h in enumerate(heights):
        start = i
        while stack and heights[stack[-1]] > h:
            idx = stack.pop()
            width = i - (stack[-1] if stack else 0)
            max_area = max(max_area, heights[idx] * width)
            start = idx
        stack.append(start)

    heights.pop()  # restore original
    return max_area
strongAnswerPoints: Maintain an increasing monotonic stack of indices; pop when a shorter bar is found and compute the rectangle immediately.
Width when popping = current index minus the new stack top (the left boundary of the rectangle).
Append a height-0 sentinel to flush all remaining bars from the stack in the main loop.
whatToAvoid: Do not attempt O(n^2) brute force — the interviewer expects the O(n) monotonic stack solution and will probe for it explicitly.
ytQuery: Largest Rectangle in Histogram monotonic stack LeetCode Amazon

question: Course Schedule II: return a valid order to complete all courses given prerequisite pairs.
summary: Topological sort via DFS with cycle detection or BFS Kahn's algorithm to find a linear ordering of directed acyclic graph nodes.
whatTheyTest: Topological sort implementation (both DFS post-order and BFS in-degree Kahn's), cycle detection in a directed graph, and graph construction from edge list.
explanation:
Model courses as nodes and prerequisites as directed edges. A valid course order exists if and only if the directed graph has no cycle (is a DAG). Topological sort gives the linear ordering.

DFS approach: color each node as WHITE (unvisited), GRAY (in current DFS path), or BLACK (fully processed). If DFS visits a GRAY node, a cycle exists — return []. When a node finishes (all its neighbors processed), add it to the result. Reverse the result to get topological order.

Kahn's BFS approach: compute in-degree for each node. Initialize a queue with all zero-in-degree nodes (no prerequisites). Process the queue: dequeue a node, add to result, decrement in-degrees of neighbors; if a neighbor's in-degree hits 0, enqueue it. If the final result length equals the number of courses, return it; otherwise a cycle exists — return [].

Both approaches are O(V + E). Kahn's is more intuitive and does not require three-color bookkeeping. The DFS approach naturally reveals the cycle path if needed.
codeExample:
from collections import deque

def find_order(num_courses, prerequisites):
    graph = [[] for _ in range(num_courses)]
    in_degree = [0] * num_courses
    for course, pre in prerequisites:
        graph[pre].append(course)
        in_degree[course] += 1

    queue = deque(i for i in range(num_courses) if in_degree[i] == 0)
    order = []
    while queue:
        node = queue.popleft()
        order.append(node)
        for neighbor in graph[node]:
            in_degree[neighbor] -= 1
            if in_degree[neighbor] == 0:
                queue.append(neighbor)

    return order if len(order) == num_courses else []
strongAnswerPoints: Use Kahn's BFS with in-degree tracking: start from nodes with no prerequisites and propagate.
A cycle is detected when the output length is less than num_courses — some nodes could never reach in-degree 0.
Build an adjacency list from the edge list first before running any traversal.
whatToAvoid: Do not forget to check for cycles — returning a partial order silently is a bug; always validate that all courses appear in the output.
ytQuery: Course Schedule II topological sort BFS DFS LeetCode Amazon

question: Word Search: determine if a word exists in a 2D grid of characters by moving horizontally or vertically.
summary: Backtracking DFS on a grid that temporarily marks cells visited and restores them on backtrack.
whatTheyTest: Backtracking pattern, in-place visited marking with restoration, and DFS termination conditions.
explanation:
For each cell matching the first character of the word, launch a DFS. At each step, if the current character matches word[index], mark the cell as visited (replace with a sentinel like '#') and recurse into all four neighbors for the next character. After the recursive call returns (whether successful or not), restore the cell to its original character — this is the 'backtrack' step.

The base case is when index == len(word): all characters matched, return True. The failure case is when the current cell is out of bounds, already visited, or does not match word[index].

Time complexity is O(M*N*4^L) where M*N is the grid size and L is word length — each DFS explores up to 4 branches for each of the L characters. In practice, early termination prunes most paths. Space is O(L) for the recursion stack. To handle the follow-up 'Word Search II' (multiple words), use a Trie of all target words to prune DFS branches where no word in the Trie can be formed.
strongAnswerPoints: Mark cells with a sentinel before recursing and restore them after — this avoids a separate visited matrix.
Return True immediately on a successful path to short-circuit remaining DFS branches.
For Word Search II with multiple words, mention Trie-based pruning to avoid re-doing DFS for every word.
whatToAvoid: Do not forget to restore the cell after backtracking — leaving sentinel values corrupts the board for other starting positions.
ytQuery: Word Search backtracking DFS LeetCode Amazon grid

question: Binary Tree Zigzag Level Order Traversal: return levels alternating left-to-right and right-to-left.
summary: BFS level-order traversal using a deque, appending to left or right of each level's result based on depth parity.
whatTheyTest: BFS level-order traversal, deque usage for O(1) front and back insertion, and tracking level direction.
explanation:
Standard BFS gives level-order left-to-right. To zigzag, track whether the current level should be left-to-right or right-to-left. Use a deque for each level's values: on even levels (left-to-right), append each value to the right; on odd levels (right-to-left), append each value to the left. The BFS queue itself processes nodes in the same order regardless — only the output collection changes.

An alternative is to reverse alternate levels after collecting them, but this adds O(n) per level. The deque approach achieves O(1) per insertion. Both are O(n) overall.

A clean implementation uses a single BFS queue (standard deque) for node processing and a separate list (converted to deque on insertion) for the current level's output. After processing all nodes at a level, append the level list to results and flip the direction flag.
strongAnswerPoints: Use a deque for each level's output: appendleft for right-to-left levels, append for left-to-right levels.
The BFS queue processes nodes in the same order always — only the output insertion point changes per level.
Toggle a boolean direction flag after each level completes — simpler than tracking level index parity.
whatToAvoid: Do not reverse the list after collecting each level — use deque insertion direction instead to avoid the extra O(level_size) reversal.
ytQuery: Binary Tree Zigzag Level Order Traversal BFS deque LeetCode

question: Find Median from Data Stream: design a data structure that returns the median after each integer insertion.
summary: Two-heap design using a max-heap for the lower half and a min-heap for the upper half to maintain O(log n) insertion and O(1) median.
whatTheyTest: Two-heap balancing pattern, understanding of heap invariants, and O(log n) insertion with O(1) median retrieval.
explanation:
The key insight is to maintain two heaps: a max-heap (small) holding the lower half of numbers, and a min-heap (large) holding the upper half. Invariant: small.size == large.size (even count) or small.size == large.size + 1 (odd count). The median is small.top() for odd count, or average of small.top() and large.top() for even count.

On each insertion: push the new element to small (max-heap). If the new element is larger than the smallest element in large, move the max of small to large (to maintain ordering). Rebalance: if large has more elements than small, move large's min to small. If small has more than large by more than 1, move small's max to large.

Python's heapq is a min-heap, so simulate a max-heap by negating values. Each insertion is O(log n) due to heap push/pop. Median retrieval is O(1) by reading heap tops. This is optimal for a streaming median — sorting on every query would be O(n log n) per query.
strongAnswerPoints: Max-heap for lower half and min-heap for upper half ensures the median is always accessible in O(1) at the heap tops.
Maintain size invariant: small.size equals large.size or large.size + 1 — rebalance after every insertion.
In Python, negate values to simulate a max-heap with heapq (which is a min-heap).
whatToAvoid: Do not maintain a sorted list and insert in sorted order — that is O(n) per insertion and fails at scale for a data stream.
ytQuery: Find Median from Data Stream two heaps LeetCode Amazon hard

question: 3Sum: find all unique triplets in an array that sum to zero.
summary: Sort the array then use a fixed outer pointer with two inner pointers to find pairs summing to the negative of each element.
whatTheyTest: Two-pointer technique after sorting, duplicate skipping logic, and reducing a 3-variable problem to a 2-variable sub-problem.
explanation:
Sort the array first. For each index i, the problem reduces to finding two numbers in nums[i+1:] that sum to -nums[i] — a Two Sum variant solvable with two pointers in O(n). Fix i, place left = i+1 and right = n-1. If the sum of the three is zero, record the triplet, advance both pointers skipping duplicates. If the sum is too small, increment left; if too large, decrement right.

Duplicate triplets are avoided by skipping repeated values at both the outer loop (if nums[i] == nums[i-1], skip) and the inner pointers (after recording a result, skip all identical values for left and right). This ensures each unique triplet appears exactly once without a set-based deduplication.

Time complexity is O(n log n) for sort plus O(n^2) for the nested scan, giving O(n^2) overall. Space is O(1) ignoring the output. A hash-set approach for the inner Two Sum is also O(n^2) but with O(n) extra space and harder duplicate handling — the two-pointer approach is cleaner.
strongAnswerPoints: Sort first — this enables the two-pointer approach and makes duplicate detection trivial.
Skip duplicate outer elements (nums[i] == nums[i-1]) to avoid producing the same triplet from different starting positions.
After recording a valid triplet, skip all duplicate left and right values before advancing the pointers.
whatToAvoid: Do not use a set of tuples for deduplication — it works but adds O(n) space and sorting overhead; the skip-duplicate pointer approach is O(1) extra space and cleaner.
ytQuery: 3Sum LeetCode two pointers sort Amazon medium

question: LRU Cache: design a data structure with O(1) get and put operations and least-recently-used eviction.
summary: Combine a hash map with a doubly linked list to achieve O(1) access, insertion, and eviction simultaneously.
whatTheyTest: Combined data structure design, doubly linked list pointer manipulation, and understanding of why both a map and a list are needed together.
explanation:
A hash map alone gives O(1) lookup but cannot efficiently track usage order. A doubly linked list alone maintains order but gives O(n) lookup. The LRU cache combines both: the map stores key → node (for O(1) lookup) and the doubly linked list maintains recency order (most recent at head, least recent at tail).

Get: look up the node in the map, move it to the head (most recently used), return its value. Put: if key exists, update value and move to head. If key is new, insert a new node at the head; if capacity is exceeded, remove the tail node (least recently used) and delete it from the map.

Using sentinel head and tail dummy nodes eliminates edge-case code for inserting into or removing from an empty list. Python's OrderedDict gives the same semantics built-in: move_to_end() and popitem(last=False) mimic the doubly linked list operations. For an interview, implementing the full doubly linked list version demonstrates deeper understanding.
strongAnswerPoints: HashMap gives O(1) key lookup; doubly linked list gives O(1) move-to-front and remove-from-tail.
Sentinel head and tail nodes eliminate edge cases for empty list operations.
Store the key inside each node so the evicted tail node's key can be deleted from the map.
whatToAvoid: Do not use a singly linked list — you need O(1) removal of arbitrary nodes, which requires access to the predecessor node that only a doubly linked list provides.
ytQuery: LRU Cache design doubly linked list hash map LeetCode Amazon

question: Merge Intervals: given a list of intervals, merge all overlapping intervals.
summary: Sort intervals by start time, then greedily merge each interval with the last merged interval if they overlap.
whatTheyTest: Interval manipulation, sorting by start time, and the greedy merging criterion based on comparing end times.
explanation:
Sort intervals by start time. Initialize the result list with the first interval. For each subsequent interval, compare its start with the end of the last merged interval. If start <= last_end, they overlap — update last_end to max(last_end, current_end) (the max handles the case where the current interval is entirely inside the last). If start > last_end, no overlap — append the current interval as a new entry.

Sorting is O(n log n) and the single pass scan is O(n), so overall O(n log n). Space is O(n) for the output.

Common follow-up: insert a new interval into an already-merged sorted list (LeetCode 57). Iterate the existing intervals in three phases: add all intervals ending before the new interval's start; merge all overlapping intervals with the new interval by expanding its start and end; add all remaining intervals. This is O(n) since no sorting is needed.
strongAnswerPoints: Sort by start time first — this ensures overlapping intervals are always adjacent in the sorted order.
Use max(last_end, current_end) when merging to handle the case where the new interval is fully contained within the last.
Mention the Insert Interval follow-up (LeetCode 57) which is O(n) because the list is pre-sorted.
whatToAvoid: Do not sort by end time — sorting by start time is the correct approach and is what makes the greedy single-pass work.
ytQuery: Merge Intervals LeetCode sort greedy Amazon phone screen

question: Serialize and Deserialize a Binary Tree: convert a tree to a string and reconstruct it exactly.
summary: Preorder DFS serialization with null markers enables unambiguous reconstruction without needing two traversals.
whatTheyTest: Tree serialization format design, preorder traversal, and recursive deserialization using an iterator over the token stream.
explanation:
A preorder traversal (root, left, right) with explicit null markers ('N' or '#') uniquely defines the tree structure. Serialize recursively: if the node is null, output 'N'; otherwise output the node's value, then serialize left and right subtrees. Join tokens with a delimiter like ','.

Deserialization uses an iterator over the split token list. Read the next token: if 'N', return None (base case). Otherwise create a node with that value, then recursively build its left and right subtrees from the same iterator. The iterator's position in the token stream tracks where we are in the reconstruction — no index arithmetic needed.

Level-order (BFS) serialization is the format LeetCode uses for displaying trees. It requires storing null markers for missing children of non-leaf nodes. Preorder DFS is generally simpler to implement and reconstruct. Neither approach works for a BST if you want to omit null markers — that would require both inorder and preorder traversals to reconstruct uniquely.
strongAnswerPoints: Preorder DFS with explicit null markers ('N') is self-describing — the structure is unambiguous without needing a second traversal.
Use Python's iter() and next() on the token list for deserialization — no index tracking needed.
Null markers are required; omitting them makes reconstruction ambiguous for non-BST trees.
whatToAvoid: Do not try to serialize without null markers using a single traversal — you cannot uniquely reconstruct a general binary tree from only one traversal without null markers.
ytQuery: Serialize Deserialize Binary Tree preorder DFS LeetCode Amazon hard

question: Next Permutation: rearrange numbers into the lexicographically next greater permutation in-place.
summary: Find the rightmost descent, swap with the next greater element to its right, then reverse the suffix to get the next permutation.
whatTheyTest: In-place array manipulation, understanding of lexicographic ordering, and the three-step algorithm for next permutation.
explanation:
The algorithm has three steps. First, scan from right to left to find the largest index i such that nums[i] < nums[i+1] — this is the 'rightmost ascent'. If no such index exists, the entire array is in descending order (the last permutation); reverse it to get the first permutation and return.

Second, scan from right to left to find the largest index j such that nums[j] > nums[i]. Swap nums[i] and nums[j]. After the swap, nums[i] is now the next-larger digit at that position.

Third, reverse the suffix starting at nums[i+1]. Because the suffix was in descending order (by how we found i), reversing it gives the smallest ascending arrangement, ensuring the overall permutation is just barely larger than the original.

All three steps are O(n) and the algorithm is O(1) space. This exact algorithm is used in combinatorics to enumerate all permutations in lexicographic order by repeatedly calling next permutation.
codeExample:
def next_permutation(nums):
    n = len(nums)
    # Step 1: find rightmost ascent
    i = n - 2
    while i >= 0 and nums[i] >= nums[i+1]:
        i -= 1

    if i >= 0:
        # Step 2: find next greater element to the right of i
        j = n - 1
        while nums[j] <= nums[i]:
            j -= 1
        nums[i], nums[j] = nums[j], nums[i]

    # Step 3: reverse suffix after i
    left, right = i + 1, n - 1
    while left < right:
        nums[left], nums[right] = nums[right], nums[left]
        left += 1
        right -= 1
strongAnswerPoints: Find the rightmost index where nums[i] < nums[i+1] — this is the pivot that must be incremented.
Swap nums[i] with the smallest element to its right that is still larger than nums[i].
Reverse the suffix after i — since it was descending, reversing makes it the smallest possible ascending arrangement.
whatToAvoid: Do not generate all permutations and sort them — that is O(n!*n) and completely impractical; the three-step in-place algorithm is O(n).
ytQuery: Next Permutation LeetCode algorithm in-place Amazon medium

question: Design Amazon's Product Recommendation System at scale.
summary: Design a recommendation engine handling billions of products and hundreds of millions of users with real-time personalization and A/B testing.
whatTheyTest: Offline model training pipelines, real-time feature stores, approximate nearest neighbor retrieval, A/B testing infrastructure, and cold start handling.
explanation:
Amazon's recommendation system separates into an offline pipeline and an online serving layer. The offline pipeline collects user-item interactions (clicks, purchases, add-to-cart, dwell time) in a data lake (S3). A Spark-based batch job trains a two-tower neural network or matrix factorization model weekly, producing item embeddings (dense vectors) stored in a vector database (FAISS or Pinecone). User embeddings are pre-computed nightly and stored in a feature store (Redis/DynamoDB).

The online serving path: when a user opens the homepage, the recommendation service fetches the user embedding from the feature store (or computes a lightweight real-time embedding from the last 10 interactions). It runs approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search to retrieve the top-500 candidate items. A lightweight re-ranking model scores candidates using real-time signals (current cart, trending items, price, inventory, personalized discounts) and returns the top-20.

Cold start for new users falls back to popularity-based rankings segmented by country and device type. Cold start for new items uses content-based features (category, title embeddings, images) until enough interactions accumulate. An A/B testing framework routes a percentage of traffic to experimental recommendation models; Bayesian online inference detects winners early and auto-promotes. Real-time user behavior tracking feeds into the feature store via a Kafka stream to keep session-level signals fresh within seconds.
strongAnswerPoints: Separate offline model training (weekly Spark jobs, two-tower model) from online serving (ANN search + re-ranking) — they scale independently.
Use a feature store for sub-10ms user embedding retrieval; pre-compute nightly and update in real time via Kafka for session signals.
Address cold start explicitly: new users get popularity-based results; new items use content embeddings until interaction data accumulates.
whatToAvoid: Do not design a single real-time training loop — model training is expensive and happens offline; only feature fetching and candidate scoring are on the real-time path.
ytQuery: Amazon recommendation system design two tower model feature store A/B testing
